question: A modification command contains an activation instruction for task `X` and a termination instruction for task `Y`, where task `Y` is currently the only active element in the process instance. After the command applies, the process instance keeps running. Why?

options.text: The engine applies all termination instructions first, then activations, so task `Y` is terminated and task `X` is created on a fresh empty instance.
The engine applies all activation instructions first, so task `X` is active before task `Y` is terminated and the process instance still has an active element.
The engine retries the termination after the activation creates a new active instance asynchronously.
The engine silently ignores the termination because applying it would otherwise end the process instance.

a.text: The documented order is the opposite — activations are applied first, then terminations.

b.text: The docs state that the engine applies all activation instructions first and all termination instructions second, so a freshly activated element prevents the cascading process-end rule from firing when the previous last active instance is removed.

c.text: There is no retry phase. Modification is a single transactional command, not a polling loop.

d.text: Modification is transactional — instructions are not silently dropped; if anything could not apply, the entire command would be rejected.

explanation: Per the docs, modification commands apply activation instructions first and termination instructions second. This ordering is what lets you swap the last active element of a process instance or a subprocess: by the time the termination runs, the activation has already produced a new active element, so the cascading "terminate the parent when empty" rule does not fire. The whole command is transactional — all or nothing.
